Question: A preadolescent girl presented to the pediatric ophthalmology clinic for evaluation and treatment of possible papilledema, discovered by an outside eye care professional during routine eye examination. Aside from mild hyperopic astigmatism, there was no significant ocular, medical, or family history. The patient denied any change in vision, scotomas, transient visual obscurations, dimming of light, photophobia, or pain. She denied headache, pulsatile tinnitus, weight gain, joint pain, lethargy, decreased appetite, fever, or chills. The patient was not taking any prescription or over-the-counter medicines; however, she had recently used a short-term course of oral antibiotics for a tooth infection.On examination, Snellen visual acuity with correction was 20/20 OD and 20/25 OS. Confrontation visual fields and ocular motility were normal. External examination was unremarkable, and slitlamp examination revealed normal eyelids, conjunctiva, and sclera. There were 1+ anterior chamber white blood cells and trace anterior vitreous cells bilaterally. Posterior segment examination revealed 360  nerve hyperemia with elevation of nasal disc borders bilaterally (Figure 1), without vitreous haze, snowballs, or snowbanks. There was no vascular sheathing, choroidal granulomas, or chorioretinal scarring in the periphery.Serum and urine testing for various inflammatory and infectious processes What Would You Do Next?
Options: A:Observation with close follow-up B:Lumbar puncture C:Serum and urine testing for various inflammatory and infectious processes D:Orbital and brain magnetic resonance imaging
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
caption: Fundus photography from initial consultation showing 360  nerve hyperemia with mild elevation of the nasal disc border bilaterally. A, Right eye. B, Left eye.
Core needle biopsy of the right kidney showing tubulointerstitial inflammation and fibrosis, with predominantly lymphocytic infiltration and scattered plasma cells and rare eosinophils (arrowheads).
opc: Serum and urine testing for various inflammatory and infectious processes
opd: Orbital and brain magnetic resonance imaging
diagnosis: Bilateral anterior uveitis after recent antibiotic use in a preadolescent girl tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis syndrome
answer: Serum and urine testing for various inflammatory and infectious processes
explanation: Bilateral iridocyclitis and papillitis in a healthy child warrants investigation. The simultaneous onset, nongranulomatous findings, and bilaterality narrow the differential diagnosis; infectious workup as well as postinfectious autoimmune or drug-induced causes, including tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis (TINU) syndrome, must be considered.1First described in 1975,2 TINU is an inflammatory process including both interstitial nephritis and uveitis. The nephritis is presumably immune mediated, secondary to medication (antibiotics), infection, or other autoimmune disease; it may also be idiopathic.3 The etiology is unknown, although common antigenicity in the renal tubular epithelium and ciliary body epithelium has been found.4Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis occurs in patients of various ethnic descent and is more prevalent in women in their second and third decades of life,3 with a second peak in the fourth decade.5 Systemic symptoms are nonspecific and include fever, malaise, or flank tenderness. The uveitis is typically bilateral and nongranulomatous, following the nephritis.3 Other ocular findings include conjunctival hyperemia, vitreous cells, and optic nerve hyperemia. Rarely, retinal vascular sheathing or hemorrhage, chorioretinitis, or pars plana exudates may be found.3Laboratory workup for TINU may reveal high urinary levels of protein, creatinine, eosinophils, glucose, and  2-microglobulin, a marker for interstitial nephritis. Serum testing may reveal an elevated creatinine level, blood urea nitrogen level, or erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Genetic positivity for HLA-DRB101 is strongly suggestive of TINU.6 A kidney biopsy may be required for diagnosis.Renal function typically improves, although persistent renal dysfunction has been reported in 11% of cases.3 Ocular disease tends to recur and is more intense with relapse.3 Steroid-sparing immunomodulatory therapy may therefore be required to extinguish the inflammation; azathioprine, methotrexate, cyclosporine, and mycophenolate mofetil are effective.3 Rarely, posterior synechiae, cataract, or ocular hypertension can occur in TINU. Overall, visual prognosis is good.This patient was prescribed topical corticosteroids and laboratory testing revealed an elevated urine  2-microglobulin (1593  g/L; normal range, 0-300  g/L) level, mildly elevated serum lysozyme level, and normal levels of angiotensin-converting enzyme, QuantiFERON gold TB test results, fluorescent treponemal antibody absorption, antinuclear antibody, rheumatoid factor, and cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. A chest radiograph was normal.At 1-week follow-up, there was a decrease in anterior chamber cells and resolution of the disc edema. The papillitis was considered to be secondary to anterior segment inflammation. Imaging of the central nervous system and lumbar puncture were therefore deferred.The patient was referred to a pediatric rheumatologist and a nephrologist. A core needle kidney biopsy was obtained showing interstitial nephritis (Figure 2), confirming the diagnosis of TINU.Core needle biopsy of the right kidney showing tubulointerstitial inflammation and fibrosis, with predominantly lymphocytic infiltration and scattered plasma cells and rare eosinophils (arrowheads).Although the patient responded to topical corticosteroids, oral steroids were required to quiet the inflammation. The urine  2-microglobulin levels eventually normalized. After a slow taper and cessation of steroid therapy, the ocular inflammation recurred and in conjunction with her rheumatologist, the patient started receiving oral methotrexate. No additional ocular involvement was noted during the course of the patient s treatment.
